reason: Computers process data; information is the processed result. Floppies/word are not things a computer 'operates on'.
Both instructions and addresses are encoded as binary codes; 'binary word' names a unit, not a representation, and parity is error checking.
EBCDIC was created by IBM for System/360 and is still used on IBM mainframes; ASCII is ANSI, Baudot is telegraphy, Hollerith is obsolete punched-card coding.

CD-ROM is read-only (pressed), not write-once, and magneto-optical is rewritable; among the write-once optical media listed, digital paper is the newest, WORM the older one. Source key 'CD-ROM disk' is factually wrong.
Videodisc's headline advantage over prior media is its very large potential storage capacity.
A 9-track tape drive has one read/write head per track = 9.
A CHS disk address identifies surface (head), track (cylinder) and sector, so all three.
Hard disks are less portable, more rigid and faster than diskettes; the only true option is more expensive.
Floppy disk and diskette are synonyms.
Poorly formed: A, C and D are all true. A VDU/terminal historically includes a keyboard so it is not purely an output device; the broadest statement that is always correct is 'peripheral device'.
A terminal producing paper output is by definition a hard copy terminal; a plotter is a device, not a terminal.
Dot-matrix is an impact printer type (option labels in this item are A/E/F/G).
Main (primary) memory is divided into ROM and RAM; primary/secondary is the wider storage split, not a split of main memory.
Spraying electrically/magnetically charged ink droplets to form a dot matrix describes an ink-jet printer.
Desktop publishing needs high-resolution page output, which is laser printing.
Producing audio output from prerecorded words is a voice response unit; a voice recognition unit is input.
A write to a memory location is destructive: it overwrites/erases the previous content.
A buffer is storage that compensates for speed differences between units.
